sections.title: Du-Form: Informal Singular Commands
sections.explanation: To form the du-imperative, take the du-form of the present tense and drop the -st ending and the pronoun. For most verbs: du gehst → Geh! du kommst → Komm! du machst → Mach! If the verb stem ends in -d, -t, -ig, or a consonant cluster, keep the -e: du arbeitest → Arbeite! du öffnest → Öffne! Verbs with an e→i or e→ie stem change keep the change but drop the -e ending: du gibst → Gib! du liest → Lies! Verbs with a→ä do NOT keep the umlaut: du fährst → Fahr! (not Fähr!)
sections.tip: Quick rule: Take the du-form, drop -st, drop 'du'. Stem-changing e→i/ie verbs keep the change. But a→ä verbs lose the umlaut!

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Come!' (informal, to one person) in German?
exercises.explanation: Du-imperative of 'kommen': drop -st from 'du kommst' and remove 'du' → Komm!

exercises.prompt: ___ die Hausaufgaben! (Do the homework!) — use the du-form imperative of 'machen'
exercises.explanation: Du-imperative of 'machen': du machst → Mach! Mach die Hausaufgaben!

exercises.prompt: What is the du-imperative of 'geben' (to give)? Remember: e→i stem change.
exercises.explanation: 'geben' has an e→i stem change (du gibst), which is kept in the imperative: Gib! (not Geb!)

exercises.prompt: What is the du-imperative of 'fahren' (to drive)? Remember: a→ä verbs.
exercises.explanation: Verbs with a→ä change do NOT keep the umlaut in the imperative: Fahr! (not Fähr!)

sections.title: Ihr-Form: Informal Plural Commands
sections.explanation: The ihr-imperative is the simplest form: it is identical to the ihr-form of the present tense, but without the pronoun 'ihr'. ihr geht → Geht! ihr kommt → Kommt! ihr macht → Macht! ihr sprecht → Sprecht! This form is used when giving a command to a group of people you address informally.
sections.tip: Ihr-imperative = present tense ihr-form minus 'ihr'. That's it!

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Wait!' to a group of friends (ihr-form)?
exercises.explanation: Ihr-imperative of 'warten': ihr wartet → Wartet! (The extra -e- stays because the stem ends in -t.)

exercises.prompt: ___ leise! (Be quiet! — to a group of children) — use the ihr-form imperative of 'sein'
exercises.explanation: Ihr-imperative of 'sein': ihr seid → Seid! Seid leise!

sections.title: Sie-Form: Formal Commands
sections.explanation: The formal imperative (Sie-form) is used for people you address with 'Sie'. It is formed by placing the verb before 'Sie': Sie gehen → Gehen Sie! Sie kommen → Kommen Sie! Sie nehmen → Nehmen Sie! The pronoun 'Sie' is always included and always capitalized. The verb form is identical to the present tense Sie-form (which is the same as the infinitive for all verbs except 'sein').
sections.tip: Sie-imperative = verb + Sie + rest of sentence. Always include 'Sie'! The verb form matches the infinitive (except sein → seien).

examples.de: Geben Sie mir bitte den Schlüssel!

exercises.prompt: How do you formally say 'Please wait!' in German?
exercises.explanation: Sie-imperative: verb first, then 'Sie'. Warten Sie bitte! The pronoun must be included.

exercises.prompt: ___ Sie das Fenster! (Open the window! — formal) — use the imperative of 'öffnen'
exercises.hint: Sie-form uses the infinitive form of the verb
exercises.explanation: Sie-imperative of 'öffnen': Öffnen Sie das Fenster!

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct formal imperative for 'to read' (lesen)?
exercises.explanation: The Sie-form imperative always uses the infinitive-based form (no stem change): Lesen Sie den Brief! Stem changes only apply to du-form.

sections.title: Irregular Imperatives
sections.explanation: A few verbs have irregular imperative forms. The most important is 'sein' (to be): du-form → Sei! ihr-form → Seid! Sie-form → Seien Sie! 'haben' (to have) uses: du-form → Hab! ihr-form → Habt! Sie-form → Haben Sie! 'werden' (to become) uses: du-form → Werde! ihr-form → Werdet! Sie-form → Werden Sie! 'sein' is the only verb where the Sie-imperative does not match the infinitive (Seien Sie, not Sein Sie).
sections.tip: The key irregular imperative to memorize is 'sein': Sei still! (Be quiet!) / Seid nett! (Be nice!) / Seien Sie vorsichtig! (Be careful!)

examples.en: Don't be afraid! (lit: Have no fear!)

exercises.prompt: Match each imperative form of 'sein' to the correct addressee:
exercises.explanation: sein: Sei! (du), Seid! (ihr), Seien Sie! (Sie). 'Seien' is the only Sie-imperative that differs from the infinitive.

exercises.prompt: ___ keine Angst! (Don't be afraid! — to a friend) — use the du-form imperative of 'haben'
exercises.explanation: Du-imperative of 'haben': Hab! Hab keine Angst!

exercises.prompt: What is the formal (Sie) imperative of 'sein'?
exercises.explanation: 'sein' is unique: the Sie-imperative is 'Seien Sie' (not 'Sein Sie'). This is the only verb with this irregularity.

sections.title: Imperatives with Separable Verbs
sections.explanation: Separable verbs split in the imperative just like in present tense statements. The prefix goes to the end of the sentence. aufstehen (to get up): Steh auf! / Steht auf! / Stehen Sie auf! zumachen (to close): Mach zu! / Macht zu! / Machen Sie zu! mitkommen (to come along): Komm mit! / Kommt mit! / Kommen Sie mit! anfangen (to begin): Fang an! / Fangt an! / Fangen Sie an!
sections.tip: Separable prefix always goes to the end: Steh auf! Mach zu! Komm mit!

exercises.hint: The prefix 'auf' goes to the end
exercises.explanation: Separable verb 'aufstehen': du-imperative → Steh auf! The prefix separates to the end.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Come along!' (informal plural, from 'mitkommen')?
exercises.explanation: Separable verb 'mitkommen': ihr-imperative → Kommt mit! The prefix 'mit' separates to the end.

exercises.prompt: How do you formally say 'Please begin!' (from 'anfangen')?
exercises.explanation: Sie-imperative of 'anfangen': Fangen Sie bitte an! The prefix 'an' goes to the end, and 'bitte' sits between verb and prefix.

sections.title: Common Imperative Expressions
sections.explanation: Many everyday German expressions use the imperative. Here are the most useful ones: Komm her! (Come here!) Mach schnell! (Hurry up!) Setz dich! (Sit down! — reflexive) Pass auf! (Watch out! — separable) Hör zu! (Listen! — separable) Guck mal! (Look! — with 'mal' to soften) Sag mal! (Tell me! — with 'mal') Lass mich in Ruhe! (Leave me alone!) Halt die Klappe! (Shut up! — very informal) Keine Sorge! (Don't worry! — no verb). Adding 'mal' or 'bitte' softens a command: 'Komm mal her!' sounds friendlier than 'Komm her!'
sections.tip: Add 'mal' to soften a command (Komm mal her!). Add 'bitte' to be polite (Komm bitte her!). Add 'doch' for emphasis or encouragement (Setz dich doch!).

exercises.prompt: Match each German imperative expression to its English meaning:
exercises.explanation: These are among the most common imperative expressions in everyday German.

exercises.prompt: ___ dich! (Sit down! — informal singular, reflexive verb 'sich setzen')
exercises.explanation: Reflexive imperative of 'sich setzen': Setz dich! The reflexive pronoun changes to 'dich' for du-form.

exercises.prompt: Which word can you add to soften a command in German?
exercises.explanation: Adding 'mal' softens an imperative: 'Komm mal her!' (Come here, would you?) sounds friendlier than 'Komm her!' (Come here!)
